response:
The error you're encountering is due to a version mismatch between the WPF assembly your application is trying to load and the one available on the system. When running on Windows, the application expects the PresentationFramework assembly version 4.0.0.0, but it's not found. This typically happens when the application is targeting a newer version of .NET that doesn't include the older WPF assemblies by default. Since you're using .NET 7, which is a .NET Core-based runtime, it doesn't include the full .NET Framework assemblies like PresentationFramework. This means that the application is not self-contained and relies on the system's installed assemblies, which may not be present or may be a different version.

To resolve this issue, you need to ensure that the necessary WPF assemblies are included in your application's output. One way to do this is by using the `Publish` feature in .NET, which allows you to create a self-contained deployment. This will embed all required dependencies, including the WPF assemblies, into the output directory. By doing so, the application will not rely on the system's installed assemblies and will instead use the ones it includes. This approach is ideal for cross-platform applications as it ensures consistency across different environments.

Cousin to this, you can also modify your project file to include the necessary WPF assemblies explicitly. This can be done by adding a reference to the specific version of the PresentationFramework assembly in your project. However, this might not be necessary if you're using the self-contained deployment method. Another alternative is to use the `--self-contained` flag when publishing your application, which will bundle all required dependencies, including WPF, into the output. This ensures that the application runs correctly on any Windows machine, regardless of the installed .NET versions.

In addition to using self-contained deployment, you should also consider the implications of running WPF on macOS. Since WPF is not natively supported on macOS, you'll need to use a different UI framework, such as Cocoa, for the Mac version of your application. This means that the WPF project should not be compiled or included in the Mac build. Instead, the main application should conditionally load the appropriate UI framework based on the platform. This approach ensures that the application remains cross-platform while avoiding conflicts between different UI frameworks.
